coredata.dc:title: Antioxidant-oxidant dual action of carbon dots obtained through thermal processing of citric acid
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Olia, Federico
Fiori, Federico
Innocenzi, Plinio
coredata.dc:description: Oxidative stress caused by reactive oxygen species is at the origin of several diseases, creating a need for a compelling new generation of antioxidant agents. This study reports on the synthesis of carbon dots (C-dots) from citric acid and evaluates their antioxidant properties. C-dots were obtained via a solventless thermal degradation of citric acid at 240°C studying the process with in-situ temperature resolved infrared spectroscopy. The blue-emitting fluorescent C-dots have an average dimension of 10 nm and exhibit antioxidant and oxidant activities. This surprising dual nature, in particular the ability of generating singlet oxygen under UV irradiation, was confirmed by colorimetric assays. The citric acid C-dots exhibited a radical scavenging efficiency of 90 % and retained the antioxidant activity for over six days, demonstrating high stability in aqueous environments and showing a better long-term stability than traditional antioxidants like ascorbic acid. These results emphasize the potential of citric acid C-dots in reducing the oxidative stress in different environments. These findings underscore the potential of citric acid-derived C-dots as versatile, stable, and cost-effective materials for mitigating oxidative stress. Moreover, when photo-stimulated by UV light, these C-dots behave as oxidant agent for singlet oxygen generation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Citric acid
Carbon dots
Antioxidant
Radical scavenging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Thermogravimetric (TGA) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) analysis of citric acid from 25 up to 500°C in N2. TGA (black curve) and its first derivative (blue curve); DSC (red curve).

a) Atomic force microscopy image of the C-dots. Three different profiles have been recorded, 1, 2 and 3, indicated by the white lines in the figures. The corresponding profiles are shown in the bottom. b) Dynamic light scattering analyses of the C-dots in an aqueous solution at a concentration of 1 mg mL−1.

a) FTIR absorption spectra of citric acid (CA) (black line) and the C-dot (red line) in the 4000 – 2000 cm−1 interval. b) FTIR absorption spectra of citric acid (black line) and the C-dot (red line) in the 2000–500 cm−1 interval.

In situ temperature resolved FTIR absorption spectra of citric acid. a) FTIR spectra in the 2200–1450 cm−1 range from 25 up to 240°C. b) 3D map of the temperature resolved spectra, x (wavenumber) – y (temperature) – z (absorbance in false colour scale). The dotted line marks the citric acid decomposition process.

a) Fluorescence map (300 – 700 nm), (emission (x), excitation (y), intensity (false color scale)), of C-dots. b) Photoluminescence (PL) decay of C-dots, analyzed through time-resolved fluorescence measurements. The experimental data (blue dots) were modelled using a bi-exponential fit (red line).
a) UV-Vis absorption spectrum of C-dots in aqueous solution. The spectrum of citric acid (CA) is shown in the inset; b) UV-Vis absorption spectrum of C-dots at different concentrations in water.
a) DPPH assay of L-ascorbic acid, as prepared and after 3 days of storage in water in the dark; b) DPPH assay of C-dots as prepared and after 3 and 6 days of storage in water in the dark.

ROS generation test. a) Aqueous solution of ICG and citric acid C-dots irradiated at 365 nm for different steps of 5 min. b) Slope curves of only ICG and ICG + Citric acid C-dots obtained by studying the decrease in the 782 nm band at after different irradiations of 5 minutes at 365 nm (normalized by max).
The processing steps to obtain C-dots from citric acid.
Graphical representation of interaction between free radicals and C-dot surface.

head.ce:title: Antioxidant-oxidant dual action of carbon dots obtained through thermal processing of citric acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Materials Science and Nanotechnology (LMNT), CR-INSTM, Department of Biomedical Sciences, University of Sassari, Viale S. Pietro 43/B, Sassari 07100, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Materials Science and Nanotechnology (LMNT), CR-INSTM, Department of Biomedical Sciences, University of Sassari

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Materials Science and Nanotechnology (LMNT), CR-INSTM, Department of Biomedical Sciences, University of Sassari, Viale S. Pietro 43/B, 07100 Sassari, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oxidative stress caused by reactive oxygen species is at the origin of several diseases, creating a need for a compelling new generation of antioxidant agents. This study reports on the synthesis of carbon dots (C-dots) from citric acid and evaluates their antioxidant properties. C-dots were obtained via a solventless thermal degradation of citric acid at 240°C studying the process with in-situ temperature resolved infrared spectroscopy. The blue-emitting fluorescent C-dots have an average dimension of 10 nm and exhibit antioxidant and oxidant activities. This surprising dual nature, in particular the ability of generating singlet oxygen under UV irradiation, was confirmed by colorimetric assays. The citric acid C-dots exhibited a radical scavenging efficiency of 90 % and retained the antioxidant activity for over six days, demonstrating high stability in aqueous environments and showing a better long-term stability than traditional antioxidants like ascorbic acid. These results emphasize the potential of citric acid C-dots in reducing the oxidative stress in different environments. These findings underscore the potential of citric acid-derived C-dots as versatile, stable, and cost-effective materials for mitigating oxidative stress. Moreover, when photo-stimulated by UV light, these C-dots behave as oxidant agent for singlet oxygen generation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Original data are available upon request to the corresponding author

ce:section.ce:para:
The application of nanomaterials in biomedical research has become increasingly widespread [1,2], and the C-dots are among the emerging materials of interest. The C-dots peculiar properties, such as excellent biocompatibility, high quantum yield fluorescence, and photostability, can be applied in sensing, imaging, and drug/gene delivery [3]. In general, C-dots are not cytotoxic, as shown by several works, and adverse effects are observed only at very high concentrations [4,5]. This property, combined with high durability and water solubility, which favors bioavailability, enlarges the field of application in biotechnologies for C-dots. In addition, several articles have been published in recent years in which C-dots with radical scavenging activity have been synthesized from different precursors [6–11]. The radical scavenging activity is exerted mostly against reactive oxygen species (ROS), which are normally generated by cellular metabolism and are essential for the performance of specific physiological cellular processes [12]. Some environmental stressors such as X-rays, UV rays, pollutants, heavy metals, and xenobiotics can promote the formation of ROS [13]. If endogenous antioxidant defenses are insufficient to counteract their accumulation, the cell is driven toward a condition of oxidative stress in which cellular components such as membrane lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids are damaged [14]. The search for a new generation of antioxidants in the form of nanomaterials is a current hot topic. The hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) mechanism in C-dots plays a critical role in their antioxidant activity, particularly in neutralizing free radicals. This mechanism involves the transfer of a hydrogen atom, including its electron, from a donor functional group of the C-dot to a reactive radical, neutralizing the radical and forming a stable product [15]. The presence of hydrogen donor functional groups and sp
2 -rich domains is a structural feature that promotes the formation of radical adducts in the C-dots [16]. In fact, electronic delocalization in the conjugated graphene-like regions promotes the elimination of free radicals by forming a secondary adduct [17]. This property, if properly designed, makes the C-dots potential candidates for the fabrication of highly efficient antioxidant nanosystems. Among these candidates, C-dots synthesized from citric acid are of particular interest. Citric acid [18] is a weak acid widely used as a precursor for C-dots [19] that can be employed as a single reagent [20] or in combination with other molecules, depending on the desired composition and surface functional groups that are required for the design of the C-dots [21–23]. Its thermal decomposition is a complex process that forms several intermediates resulting from dehydration and reduction processes [24]. The core structure of the C-dots depends very much on the thermal treatment, and in general, higher processing temperatures favour the formation of graphitic structures [1,6]. An increase in reaction time also promotes carbonization and the reduction of the outer amorphous region [19,25−28].

In this work, we have synthesized C-dots via thermal processing using citric acid as the only precursor. The citric acid C-dots have shown excellent antioxidant activity that remains stable over time in contrast to ascorbic acid used as an antioxidant reference molecule. The C-dots have exhibited a surprisingly dual nature and can also produce ROS when illuminated by UV light. Their prolonged stability over time, ease of synthesis, and low cost of production, potentially make these C-dots ideal candidates for applications where it is necessary to counteract the formation of ROS.

Citric acid anhydrous powder (≥ 99 %, C6H8O7) was purchased from Alfa Aesar, L-Ascorbic acid (≥ 99 %, C6H8O6), 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (C18H12N5O6, DPPH), Methanol (≥ 99,9 %, CH4O) and benzoylated membranes for dialysis tubing (average flat width 32 mm, 1.27 in., molecular weight cutoff = 2000 Da) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO.

Thermogravimetric Analysis and Differential Scanning Calorimetry (TGA-DSC) analysis of citric acid were carried out with a SDT Q600 device under N2 inert atmosphere (flow rate of 20 mL min−1; ramp of 10°C min−1 up to 500°C). The powders were placed in alumina crucibles for the analysis.

Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) images of carbon dots were acquired with a NT-MDT Integra at 0.2 Hz scan speed in semicontact mode, a silicon tip with typical resonance frequency of 240 kHz was used at 11.8 N m−1 force constant.

The hydrodynamic diameter of C-dots was observed through dynamic light scattering (DLS) with a Zetasizer Nano S90 (Malvern Instruments, Westborough, MA, USA) and the data were processed with Zetasizer software version 7.12. Solid samples were dissolved in Milli-Q water at a concentration of 1 mg mL−1 and let to equilibrate for 120 seconds before measurement. Each measurement was the average of 3 runs, with 20 measurements per run. The final result was obtained by averaging three replicates.

FTIR analyses were performed using a Bruker Vertex 70 spectrophotometer in absorbance mode in the range from 4000 to 400 cm−1 with 32 scans and 4 cm−1 resolution. KBr pellets were prepared by mixing 1 mg of citric acid carbon dots in 200 mg of KBr powder. In situ FTIR analysis was performed on KBr pellets prepared with 1:5 ratio with temperature raising from 23 up to 240°C with 10°C steps increase (128 scans and 4 cm−1 resolution).

Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy measurements were performed in 3D fluorescence mapping mode using a Horiba Jobin Yvon Nanolog spectrofluorometer equipped with 450 W Xenon lamp. Carbon dots powders were dispersed in water in a concentration of 0.025 mg mL−1 and analysed in an excitation/emission range of 275–700 nm keeping the slits at 3 nm and with a wavelength increment of 5 nm. Fluorescence intensities were reported in a false colour scale. The data were collected at t0, after 3 days and 6 days of storage in water.

Quantum yield (QY%) was measured with Quanta-ϕ (HORIBA) integrating sphere, connected to the same spectrofluorometer. Measurements were performed in aqueous solutions of the samples at concentration of 0.025 mg mL−1. Samples were excited with a wavelength corresponding to their maximum excitation peak (365 nm) and emissions were recorded from 200 to 500 nm. The absolute external quantum yield (QY) was calculated using the following equations:
(1)
QY(%) = P
1
/ P
0
x 100
Where P1 is the integrated intensity of the emitted photons and P0 of the incident photons,
The Horiba Jobin Yvon Nanolog spectrofluorometer same was also used to measure the fluorescence lifetime with a NanoLED-340 as excitation light sources (λ = 340 nm, pulse duration<1.2 ns).
UV-Vis spectra of C-dots and citric acid were acquired at t0, 3 and 6 days using a Nicolet Evolution 300 spectrophotometer in the range from 200 to 600 nm and with a bandwidth of 1.5 nm.

Anhydrous citric acid (4 g, 0.02 mol) was placed into a ceramic crucible and heated in an oven at 240°C for 3 h. After thermal treatment the sample was left to cool down for 15 minutes out of the oven. The brown-black solid product was grounded with a mortar until a brownish powder was obtained. The powder was suspended in 5 mL of ultrapure water, and it was sonicated for 45 minutes. After sonication the brownish solution was collected and centrifugated at 9000 rpm for 20 minutes. The supernatant was collected and filtered with 0.22 µm filter. After filtration the sample was dialyzed for 72 h, freeze-dried, and the products collected ( Scheme 1).

A 0.25 mM methanol solution of DPPH was prepared by dissolving 1 mg of DPPH in 10.5 mL of MeOH. A 1:1 working solution was prepared by adding 300 µL of the methanol solution of DPPH to 300 µL of water solution of carbon dots and stored in the dark for 1 h. Then, the absorbance at 517 nm was acquired. For the determination of radical scavenging activity, the following formula was applied:
(2)
Radical scavenging activity (%) = (A
517
C -A
517
S)/(A
517
C) x 100
Where A517C is the absorbance value of the control at 517 nm and A517S is the absorbance value of the sample at 517 nm.
L-Ascorbic acid was used as standard scavenger for DPPH radical and it was tested at different concentrations from 0.78125 to 50 μg mL−1.
Radical scavenging activity against DPPH was evaluated for different concentrations of carbon dots: 7.81, 15.62, 31.25, 62.50, 125 and 250 μg mL−1.
Control was prepared by mixing 300 µL of DPPH methanol solution with 300 µL ultrapure water. Blank was obtained by mixing 300 µL of methanol and 300 µL of ultrapure water.

Indocyanine green (ICG) was used to evaluate the light-induced generation of singlet oxygen (1O2) by carbon dots. A water solution with a concentration of 125 μg mL−1 of C-dots and 5 μg mL−1 of ICG was placed in a 3 mL quartz cuvette. The solution was kept under stirring and irradiated with a 365 nm wavelength using a Horiba Jobin Yvon NanoLog spectrofluorometer with a 450 W Xenon lamp, operated in Real Time Control (RTC) mode with the slits set to 10 nm. The UV-Vis spectrum of the solution was then acquired in the range from 200 to 900 nm every 5 minutes up to 60 minutes. The photoinduced degradation of ICG was analysed by plotting the normalized absorbance at 782 nm as a function of time (minutes). The photostability of control ICG was performed with the same protocol.

The synthesis of C-dots from citric acid has been done through a single thermal treatment in air. The method is simple, even if the purification step has to be performed with care, requiring a careful design of the treatment temperature. Thermal analysis has been used to identify the proper thermal process to be used in the synthesis.

Fig. 1 shows the TGA, DSC and the first derivatives of TGA curve. The TGA curve shows a single-step with sudden weight loss at 214° C, due to the thermal decomposition of citric acid [29]. At temperatures higher than 300°C only less than 10 % of the original weight is left. The DSC profile shows a main endothermic event peaking at 150°C, not accompanied by a weight loss, which is attributed to the melting of citric acid [30]. After melting other two overlapped endothermic events are observed at 180 and 195°C. They are related to citric acid decomposition, which is multi-step process, with formation of different by-products [24]. Based on the thermal analysis, a temperature of 240°C was therefore selected for the synthesis, when the decomposition of citric acid is complete.

AFM images ( Fig. 2a) show that two different populations of the C-dots form: one with an average size around 10 nm and a second one of 30 nm. The largest particles are clusters formed by the aggregation of C-dots. The analysis in solution by Dynamic Light Scattering analysis (Fig. 2b) confirms the AFM observation, and two size distributions are detected. The first of nanoparticles shows an average diameter of 4 nm. The size is the typical one for C-dots, which are usually in the range of 1–10 nm. Particles of larger size indicate the presence of aggregates with an average size of 50 nm.

The composition of C-dots has been analyzed by FTIR that is a powerful tool to identify the different species formed by thermal decomposition of citric acid. We have described in a previous article the intermediate species that can form during the thermal treatment of citric acid and we have applied in the present case the same analytical approach [24]. The thermal decomposition of citric acid is a rather complex process because can give several by products and intermediate species. The formation of such intermediates depends on several factors, such as the temperature, the atmosphere and the duration of the treatment. Citric acid derived C-dots are, therefore, formed by several molecular fluorophores that can be, at least partially, identified by combining FTIR and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 3a shows the FTIR absorption spectra of citric acid and the C-dot in the 4000 – 2500 cm−1 and Fig. 3b in the 2000 – 1000 cm−1 ranges.

Citric acid in the 3500–3000 cm−1 region shows two absorption bands peaking at 3501 and 3296 cm−1 (Fig. 3a). The bands are sharp and are assigned to OH stretching and CH stretching vibration, respectively. In the C-dots, the two bands that are the molecular signature of citric acid are no longer detected. A wide band peaking around 3435 cm−1 is, instead detected. This band is due to absorbed water (OH stretching) and indicates, together with the disappearance of the sharp citric acid bands, the proceed of the amidation process with condensation of CA [31]. At lower wavenumbers (Fig. 3b) the spectrum of citric acid shows two absorption bands, at 1751 and 1705 cm−1, which are assigned to the CO stretching mode and are also the signature of anhydrous citric acid. The spectrum of the C-dot shows a new absorption band at 1624 cm−1, while the other two bands change in relative intensity and shift to higher wavenumbers, 1766 and 1718 cm−1, with respect to anhydrous citric acid. The 1624 cm−1 is assigned to CC stretching and is the indication of formation of first intermediate species, itaconic acid, trans aconitic acid, and citraconic acid, together with residual citric acid [24]. The formation of the second intermediate, citraconic anhydride, can be ruled out because the typical absorption band around 1850 cm−1 due to CO antisymmetric stretching, it is not observed. In general, as previously observed the formation of the second intermediates requires longer reaction times.

The temperature resolved infrared analysis of citric acid gives a direct indication of the changes induced by the thermal treatment to form the C-dots (
Fig. 4). In accordance with the thermal analysis it is observed that around 160°C, when citric acid melts the first intermediates form.

The C-dots obtained via thermal treatment of citric acid in air are fluorescent, even if the quantum yield is not high, 1.2 %. The fluorescence map shows that the emission is peaking around 470 nm upon excitation at 370 nm; the map is in accordance with the fluorescence observed in C-dots obtained by first intermediates, such as itaconic acid, trans aconitic acid, and citraconic acid [24]. This finding indicates that the citric acid C-dots obtained via thermal treatment of three hours at 240°C are composed by a combination of such fluorophores.

The photoluminescence decay follows a bi-exponential model:
(3)
y = A
1 exp (−t/t 1
) + A
2 exp (−t/t 2
) + y
0

Two decay times are shown: t1 = 1.39 ns (fast component) and t2 = 4.43 ns (slow component). The fast decay is attributed to non-radiative processes from surface defects, while the slower decay reflects stable radiative emission from the core of the material. The predominance of the fast component (A1 = 0.76) indicates significant influence from surface states, though the slower component (A2 = 0.36) highlights retained radiative efficiency.

The UV-spectra can also give useful information about the C-dots composition ( Fig. 6). The UV-Vis spectrum of an aqueous solution of citric acid is characterized by an intense absorption in the UV, from 200 to 250 nm (inset in Fig. 6 a). The C-dots in water show, instead, a wide absorption from 250 to 600, with a maximum around 220 nm. The spectrum is formed by several overlapped components that we have separated by mathematical deconvolution of the curve. The best fit is obtained using five components peaking at 205, 241, 279, 333 and 378 nm. This absorption bands at 205 and 241 nm are attributed to π- π* transitions of the sp
2 domains, while the absorption band at 333 nm is assigned to n - π* transitions probably arising from the CO groups present in the dots.24 The other components are due to surface defects, in particular, the band at highest wavelength, 378 nm, is the source of the fluorescence. Fig. 6b shows that increasing the concentration the absorption increases linearly indicating that the C-dots do not aggregate, at least at the concentrations used in the experiment.

One of the most interesting properties of C-dots is their capability to behave as antioxidant or oxidant systems. Several C-dots when illuminated by light are capable of generating Reactive Oxygen Species, such as singlet oxygen, otherwise if no photostimulated behave light antioxidant. This dual nature makes the C-dots particularly intriguing also because they are no toxic if not at very high concentrations and are also fluorescent. C-dots derived from citric acid have the advantage of a simple material processing that makes them a very interesting nanomaterial for bio-application. We have, therefore, tested the C-dots to check their potential use as antioxidant particles. A colorimetric test with DPPH• has been conducted for this purpose [32]. DPPH• is a free radical that is reduced in the presence of an antioxidant substance. In methanol solution, DPPH• in its oxidized form is violet. Upon its reduction, the DPPH• solution undergoes a color change to pale yellow. The color change is accompanied by a decrease in the absorption band at 517 nm, which is more marked the more the DPPH• is reduced and inhibited by the antioxidant substance [33]. As reference for the test, we have employed L-ascorbic acid, a well-known antioxidant molecule (
Fig. 7a). The scavenging effect is dose-dependent and quickly increasing with the dose. At 50 μg mL−1 the radical scavenging activity translates into 95 % inhibition of DPPH•. The test has been repeated after 3 days but after this elapsed time L-ascorbic acid lost completely the scavenging activity.

The C-dots follow a similar trend, and at the highest tested dose they are able to scavenge up to 90 % of the radicals (Fig. 7b). The antioxidant test repeated after three and six days has shown, instead, a different result because the C-dots perfectly keep their activity with time. This result is interesting because shows that C-dots have, in general an intrinsic advantage with respect to single active molecules, they are water soluble and robust, keeping their properties for a longer time and in harsher chemical environment.

The temporal stability of the antioxidant action of ascorbic acid is limited due to its chemical nature and sensitivity to environmental factors [34,35]. Ascorbic acid is prone to oxidation in the presence of oxygen, light, heat, and metal ions, leading to the formation of dehydroascorbic acid and further degradation products [36,37]. Its antioxidant efficacy typically diminishes within hours to days, depending on storage conditions [38]. In neutral solutions stored at room temperature, significant loss of activity is reported to occur within 24–48 hours. Instead, C-dots retain antioxidant activity for over six days in aqueous solutions, in contrast to the fast degradation of ascorbic acid under similar conditions. This difference in stability makes C-dots particularly advantageous for applications requiring prolonged antioxidant efficacy.

The antioxidant activity of C-dots obtained by pyrolysis of citric acid can be explained by the presence of hydrogen-donor groups, such as hydroxyl (-OH) and carboxyl (-COOH), on their surface. The conjugated sp²-carbon domains within C-dots enable electronic delocalization, which stabilizes radical intermediates formed during the HAT process. The interaction between free radicals and the functional groups on C-dots leads to the formation of stable radical adducts, effectively neutralizing the reactive oxygen species (ROS). The HAT mechanism goes through an initiation step, when a free radical, such as DPPH• used in the assay, or OH•, comes in close contact with the C-dots. This step is followed by donation of a hydrogen atom, with a surface functional group, such as -OH, which donates a hydrogen atom (proton and electron) to the free radical through the reaction:
(4) −OH + R• → −O• + RH

The resulting -O• radical (or other functional group radicals) on the C-dot is stabilized by resonance or interactions within the sp² domains ( Scheme 2). This stability minimizes further reactivity, making the C-dot itself resistant to oxidation.

Due to the robustness of the sp²-carbon core and the diversity of surface functional groups, C-dots can maintain antioxidant activity over time and in harsh conditions. Unlike traditional antioxidants, C-dots generate minimal harmful byproducts during the HAT process. For instance, ascorbic acid donates electrons or hydrogen atoms to neutralize free radicals. During this process, it converts into dehydroascorbic acid, which can further degrade into diketogulonic acid and other reactive compounds under certain conditions, potentially causing oxidative damage itself if not promptly recycled.

Besides the capability to act as an effective radical scavenger, we have tested if the citric acid C-dots are also able to generate ROS species upon UV illumination. We have used a colorimetric test with indocyanine green (ICG) to detect the ROS in an aqueous solution of C-dots. ICG is a water-soluble tricarbicyanine with a maximum absorption peak at 782 nm and a very low absorption in the UV range [39]. Exposure of ICG to singlet oxygen leads to the formation of dioxetane intermediates that further decompose into several carbonyl derivates [40].

When a mixture of ICG and C-dots (125 μg mL−1) in water is irradiated at 365 nm (the same wavelength in which C-dots is able to emit maximum fluorescence) a decrease of the band at 782 nm is observed. This is the demonstration that the C-dots are able to generate singlet oxygen after irradiation at 365 nm. After 5 minutes of irradiation at 365 nm, a decrease of 32 % of the absorbance at 782 nm is observed. After one hour of irradiation, the absorbance is 58 % lowers. It was observed that different carbon dots obtained from thermal treatment of various organic precursors, after UV irradiation, are involved in Type II reaction in which energy is transferred to molecular oxygen in triplet ground state (3O2). This energy transfer results in the spin pairing of the two electrons in the π* antibonding orbitals and in generation of highly reactive singlet oxygen species (1O2) [41,42].

This study presents the successful synthesis of carbon dots from citric acid via a simple thermal processing method. The resulting C-dots display a remarkable dual antioxidant-oxidant activity, attributed to their structural features, including sp²-rich domains and functional groups, which facilitate radical scavenging and ROS mitigation. Notably, the C-dots exhibit higher stability in aqueous solutions compared to conventional antioxidants like ascorbic acid, retaining their effectiveness over extended periods.

In addition to their antioxidant properties, the citric acid C-dots demonstrate the ability to generate singlet oxygen upon UV irradiation, suggesting their potential use in applications such as photodynamic therapy. Their fluorescence, photostability, and ease of synthesis further enhance their appeal as multifunctional nanomaterials. The findings underscore the potential of citric acid-derived C-dots as a cost-effective, sustainable, and versatile solution for biomedical and environmental applications. The C-dots due to their inborn fluorescence, can be used as fluorescent markers in bioimaging applications and, at the same time, exploited as antioxidant agents on cell cultures playing a protective effect against ROS-induced oxidative damage, for instance on lipid proteins and DNA. The ability of carbon dots to produce singlet oxygen only when subjected to UV radiation make them ideal candidates for use as photosensitizers in photodynamic therapy and antimicrobial agents. The C-dots promise to offer significant advantages, including long-term stability, robust performance, and scalability, making them an exciting prospect for future research and practical deployment.

This work has been supported by the project e.INS-Ecosystem of Innovation for Next Generation Sardinia (cod. ECS 00000038) funded by Italian Ministry for University and Research (MUR) under the National Recovery and Resilience Plan (NRRP) - MISSION 4 COMPONENT 2, "From research to business" INVESTMENT 1.5, "Creation and strengthening of Ecosystems of innovation" and construction of "Territorial R&D Leaders". The authors also acknowledge funding from MUR under the PRIN Project 2022LZWKAJ.

Federico Olia: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing and revising the original draft. Federico Fiori: Formal analysis, data analysis. Plinio Innocenzi: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Revising the draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Micromachines

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antioxidant functionalized nanoparticles: a combat against oxidative stress
Nanomater

ACS Appl. Bio Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of carbonization degree of carbon dots on cytotoxicity and photo-induced toxicity to cells
Heliyon

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Esfandiari, Z. Bagheri, H. Ehtesabi, Z. Fatahi, H. Tavana, H. Latifi. Effect of carbonization degree of carbon dots on cytotoxicity and photo-induced toxicity to cells. Heliyon, 5(12) (2019) e02940. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2019.e02940
